serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Japanese set meal serving consisting of approximately 200 g steamed white rice, 150 g grilled beef slices, 2 eggs simmered in dashi-soy broth (approximately 100 g), and 100 g accompaniments including miso soup and pickles, totalling approximately 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: Beef and egg rice bowl (gyudon-style) — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central: Beef, short loin, top loin steak (NDB 13158); Egg, whole, cooked (NDB 01129); White rice, cooked (NDB 20444); Soy sauce (NDB 16124)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for yaki beef tamago toji set. Nutrient values estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled beef (top loin, ~27% of 100 g edible portion), cooked whole egg (~18%), cooked white rice (~36%), dashi-soy broth (~14%), and green onion/condiments (~5%). Proportions derived from standard Japanese set meal composition. Energy calculated by Atwater factors. All values are per 100 g of the combined edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice, boiled (GI 64 reference); mixed beef-egg-rice meal analogue estimated at GI 54 based on protein and fat attenuation of rice GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yaki beef tamago toji set. White rice (boiled) has a GI of approximately 64–72 in the University of Sydney GI Database. The addition of substantial protein (beef, egg) and fat attenuates the glycemic response of the rice component. A mixed beef-egg-rice meal analogue GI of approximately 54 is estimated based on published data for similar Japanese rice-based set meals with protein and fat co-ingestion (Atkinson et al. 2021; Jenkins et al. mixed meal attenuation principles). GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 101.2) / 100 ≈ 54.6, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 18.4 × 5.5 = 101.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and dashi broth
hypertension.guidance: A single 550 g serving provides approximately 1705 mg sodium (310 mg per 100 g × 5.5), representing 74–85% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 54.6 per serving is moderate-to-high for a single meal; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider requesting a smaller rice portion or substituting with brown rice to reduce postprandial glucose excursion, while the protein and fat from beef and egg provide beneficial glycemic attenuation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from beef and soy-based broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving provides approximately 11.6 g saturated fat and 1705 mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose leaner beef cuts where available, as saturated fat intake above 10% of total energy is associated with increased LDL-cholesterol risk per AHA guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yaki Beef Tamago Toji Set (焼きビーフ卵とじセット) is a Japanese set meal featuring grilled (yaki) sliced beef that is finished by simmering in a sweetened dashi-soy broth with beaten eggs poured over the top — a technique called tamago toji, meaning 'egg-bound' or 'egg-simmered'. The dish is served over or alongside a bowl of steamed white rice, typically accompanied by miso soup and Japanese pickles (tsukemono). The beef is seasoned with soy sauce, mirin, and sake before grilling, giving it a savoury-sweet umami flavour with a slightly caramelised exterior. The egg toji component creates a soft, silky, custard-like coating around the beef, adding richness and binding the flavours together. Texturally, the dish contrasts the tender chew of grilled beef with the delicate softness of the egg and the fluffy steamed rice. Nutritionally, the set meal is a moderate-energy, protein-rich meal providing approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with 11.2 g protein and 18.4 g carbohydrate. The primary nutritional concerns are sodium from soy sauce and dashi broth (approximately 310 mg per 100 g), saturated fat from beef, and the moderate glycemic load from the white rice component. This dish is suitable for general consumption but warrants portion awareness for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not halal-certified in standard preparation. The dish is served at Japanese restaurants in Singapore, including GyoGyo at Funan mall.

ingredient_synonyms.mirin: sweet rice wine, Japanese cooking wine

confidence_reason: No direct entry for yaki beef tamago toji set exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients (grilled beef, cooked egg, white rice, soy sauce broth) using standard Japanese set meal proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on published mixed meal data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are per 100 g of the combined edible portion of the set meal (excluding miso soup liquid volume). Serving size of 550 g is an estimate for a standard Japanese restaurant set meal. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not directly measured for this specific dish. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient fields have been estimated from component USDA data and are reported as positive numeric values. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. Sodium is the primary clinical concern at approximately 310 mg per 100 g. The restaurant source (GyoGyo - Funan, field survey data) is preserved as provenance context only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
